behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This is pure backoff math/policy: "wait time ... doubles ... up to a configured maximum." A single function/object test catches real failures (wrong exponent, cap off-by-one) without module wiring. Rejected higher levels: no behavior text requiring real OS/network/subprocess, and the issue is a "third-party REST API" wrapper (deterministic plumbing, not model-output quality), so not System/Agentic/Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Test `computeBackoff(attempt)` directly across attempts (e.g., 1..N) and assert exponential growth with clamp at configured max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module handoff: response classification + retry policy calculation ("identifies a rate-limit response and computes ... wait time"). A unit test of either piece alone can miss wiring bugs (wrong status/header mapping into backoff). Rejected System: can reproduce with in-process fake HTTP responses/headers; no real network needed. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: behavior is REST retry plumbing, not AI output content.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry middleware with a fake transport returning 429 (and retry metadata such as reset/retry-after where supported). Assert classifier marks it retryable and next delay equals policy output.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This requires orchestration across timer/backoff + request replay + response propagation; real failures are in interaction (request body/params not preserved, result not unwrapped). Rejected System: fake clock + in-process fake transport reproduces failure without real HTTP/OS timing. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: "REST API" retry behavior is deterministic and excludes model judgment.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers and a stub transport that returns rate-limit once, then success. Assert second call receives the original request payload and `call()` resolves to the success value with no retry details leaked.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Typed error emission depends on retry-loop state reaching terminal condition and mapping to domain error. Unit-testing only constructor/type can miss loop-to-error wiring bugs. Rejected System: can force repeated 429 via stub transport, no real network required. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI/ML dependency; behavior text is deterministic retry/error handling.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries=N, stub transport to always return rate-limit, run `call()`, and assert rejection is specifically `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected metadata/cause.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly spans multiple local components ("initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking"); interaction bugs can create non-termination. Rejected System: indefinite-loop risk is reproducible with fake clock + deterministic stub transport; no OS/network primitive required. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: chain is non-LLM control flow, not agentic decisions.
behaviors.test_description: With fake timers and always-rate-limited stub, execute `call()` and advance time through all scheduled waits; assert exactly `maxRetries+1` attempts and final terminal error, with no further scheduled retries.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at shared-state boundary across concurrent callers ("multiple async callers in the same process" + "same shared quota bucket"). Unit tests of quota math alone miss race/order handoff issues between caller paths and bucket manager. Rejected System: same-process async concurrency can be reproduced in-process (Promises/microtasks) without real network/OS. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: REST quota accounting has no model-output dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/shared bucket, fire concurrent `call()` invocations via `Promise.all`, and use deterministic stub responses/counters to verify all calls consume from one common quota state rather than per-caller buckets.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: This is queueing behavior across submit path + waiting queue + dispatcher; ordering bugs are interaction defects, not single-function math. Rejected System: FIFO dispatch can be validated with fake timers and stub transport; no real HTTP/OS needed. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: behavior is deterministic scheduling in a REST client, not AI decision quality.
behaviors.test_description: Queue several calls while quota is unavailable, then release capacity and advance fake time; assert transport send order matches submission order exactly.
